summary: Joel Spolsky announces that Stack Overflow is being acquired by Prosus, a holding company, while reflecting on the slow pace of programming progress over decades. He argues that despite new frameworks and tools, building web applications remains roughly as difficult as it always was, with the notable exception of Stack Overflow itself, which genuinely changed how developers learn and get help. He reassures the community that Stack Overflow will continue to operate independently with the same team and mission. He also briefly introduces his new venture, HASH, which aims to make web data more structured and programmatically accessible.
key_points: Programming tools evolve slowly, and despite decades of progress, building CRUD web apps takes roughly the same effort it always has
Tool makers love adding features but hate removing them, leading to ever-increasing complexity without proportional productivity gains
Stack Overflow was a rare exception that genuinely changed programming overnight by transforming how developers learn and help each other
The acquisition by Prosus is structured to keep Stack Overflow operating independently with the same team, plan, and business practices
Spolsky's new venture HASH aims to solve the problem of unstructured web data that requires painful scraping before it can be used programmatically
money_quotes: COM was like Gödels Theorem: it seemed important, and you could understand it all long enough to pass an exam, but ultimately it is mostly just a demonstration of how far human intelligence can be made to stretch under extreme duress.
If there is one thing we have learned, it's that the things that make it easier on your brain are the things that matter.

I found that it took approximately the same amount of work to make a CRUD web app as it always has, and that there were some things that were, shockingly, still just as randomly difficult as they were in VBScript twenty years ago.
The biggest problem is that developers of programming tools love to add things and hate to take things away.
Six-to-eight weeks before that, Stack Overflow was only an idea. Six-to-eight weeks after that, it was a standard part of every developer's toolkit.
The company grew faster than any individual's skills at managing companies, especially mine.
Stack Overflow will continue to operate independently, with the exact same team in place that has been operating it, according to the exact same plan and the exact same business practices.
key_insight: Despite decades of programming tool evolution, genuine productivity leaps are extraordinarily rare — Stack Overflow was one, and preserving its independence and mission through the Prosus acquisition matters more than the financial event itself.
